section.title: Elihu's Fourth Speech
section.sectionRationale: Elihu's final and longest speech begins with one more defense of God's justice and disciplining purposes in affliction, then rises into an extended, awestruck description of a coming thunderstorm, rain, snow, lightning, wind, all obeying God's command without needing anyone's understanding of how. This nature hymn builds and builds until it nearly becomes the LORD's own voice; by the time God actually speaks from the whirlwind in chapter 38, the storm Elihu has been describing seems to have arrived.
section.thesis: Elihu closes his case not with a new argument but with wonder, God's mastery over rain, snow, lightning, and thunder is beyond human understanding or control, and this note of awe, more than any of Elihu's arguments, is what actually prepares Job, and the reader, for the LORD's own appearance.
section.frameNote: Two chapters, seven movements: Elihu's claim to bring perfect knowledge; God's justice toward the afflicted, using affliction to open their ears; a warning against turning toward iniquity under pressure; God's greatness displayed in the gathering storm; the obedient elements, thunder, lightning, snow, and rain; a call to stand still and consider God's wonders; and a closing confession that the Almighty is beyond our reach.

units.label: One perfect in knowledge
units.note: Elihu's claim to speak for God

verses.text: And Elihu continued:
“Bear with me a little longer, and I will show you that there is more to be said on God’s behalf.
I get my knowledge from afar, and I will ascribe justice to my Maker.
For truly my words are free of falsehood; one perfect in knowledge is with you.

units.label: Justice toward the afflicted
units.note: He opens their ears through suffering

verses.text: Indeed, God is mighty, but He despises no one; He is mighty in strength of understanding.
He does not keep the wicked alive, but He grants justice to the afflicted.
He does not take His eyes off the righteous, but He enthrones them with kings and exalts them forever.
And if men are bound with chains, caught in cords of affliction,
then He tells them their deeds and how arrogantly they have transgressed.
He opens their ears to correction and commands that they turn from iniquity.
If they obey and serve Him, then they end their days in prosperity and their years in happiness.
But if they do not obey, then they perish by the sword and die without knowledge.
The godless in heart harbor resentment; even when He binds them, they do not cry for help.
They die in their youth, among the male shrine prostitutes.
God rescues the afflicted by their affliction and opens their ears in oppression.

units.label: A warning against turning to iniquity
units.note: do not long for the night

verses.text: Indeed, He drew you from the jaws of distress to a spacious and broad place, to a table full of richness.
But now you are laden with the judgment due the wicked; judgment and justice have seized you.
Be careful that no one lures you with riches; do not let a large bribe lead you astray.
Can your wealth or all your mighty effort keep you from distress?
Do not long for the night, when people vanish from their homes.
Be careful not to turn to iniquity, for this you have preferred to affliction.

units.label: God's greatness in the gathering storm

verses.text: Behold, God is exalted in His power. Who is a teacher like Him?
Who has appointed His way for Him, or told Him, ‘You have done wrong’?
Remember to magnify His work, which men have praised in song.
All mankind has seen it; men behold it from afar.
Indeed, God is {{gadol|great}}, beyond our knowledge; the number of His years is unsearchable.
For He draws up drops of water which distill the rain from the mist,
which the clouds pour out and shower abundantly on mankind.
Furthermore, who can understand how the clouds spread out, how the thunder roars from His pavilion?
See how He scatters His lightning around Him and covers the depths of the sea.
For by these He judges the nations and provides food in abundance.
He fills His hands with lightning and commands it to strike its mark.
The thunder declares His presence; even the cattle regard the rising storm.

units.label: Thunder, lightning, snow, and rain obey
units.note: so that all men may know His work

verses.text: “At this my heart also pounds and leaps from its place.
Listen closely to the thunder of His voice and the rumbling that comes from His mouth.
He unleashes His lightning beneath the whole sky and sends it to the ends of the earth.
Then there comes a roaring sound; He thunders with His majestic voice. He does not restrain the lightning when His voice resounds.
God thunders wondrously with His voice; He does great things we cannot comprehend.
For He says to the snow, ‘Fall on the earth,’ and to the gentle rain, ‘Pour out a mighty downpour.’
He seals up the hand of every man, so that all men may know His work.
The wild animals enter their lairs; they settle down in their dens.
The tempest comes from its chamber, and the cold from the driving north winds.
By the breath of God the ice is formed and the watery expanses are frozen.
He loads the clouds with moisture; He scatters His lightning through them.
They swirl about, whirling at His direction, accomplishing all that He commands over the face of all the earth.
Whether for punishment or for His land, He accomplishes this in His {{chesed|loving devotion}}.
Listen to this, O Job; stand still and consider the {{niphlaot|wonders}} of God.
Do you know how God dispatches the clouds or makes the lightning flash?
Do you understand how the clouds float, those wonders of Him who is perfect in knowledge?
You whose clothes get hot when the land lies hushed under the south wind,
can you, like Him, spread out the skies to reflect the heat like a mirror of bronze?
Teach us what we should say to Him; we cannot draw up our case when our faces are in darkness.
Should He be told that I want to speak? Would a man ask to be swallowed up?

units.label: The Almighty is beyond our reach

verses.text: Now no one can gaze at the sun when it is bright in the skies after the wind has swept them clean.
Out of the north He comes in golden splendor; awesome majesty surrounds Him.
The Almighty is beyond our reach; He is exalted in power! In His justice and great righteousness He does not oppress.
Therefore, men {{yirah|fear}} Him, for He is not partial to the wise in heart.”

gadol.gloss: Great, large, vast. Elihu declares God gadol, great, beyond our knowledge, a claim his own nature poem then illustrates at length through the incomprehensible mechanics of rain, thunder, and lightning.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast covenant love, kindness. Elihu says God sends storms, whether for punishment or for blessing His land, in chesed, His own faithful, loving purpose, tying even the terrifying power of weather to God's covenant character.

niphlaot.gloss: Wonders, marvelous or extraordinary works, from the root pala, to be surpassing or beyond ordinary capacity. Elihu commands Job to 'stand still and consider the wonders of God,' the same word used throughout the Psalms for God's mighty and awe inspiring deeds.

yirah.gloss: Fear, reverence, awe. Elihu's closing conclusion, that all people fear God because of His inaccessible greatness and unyielding justice, sets the emotional and theological stage exactly where God's own speech from the whirlwind will begin in the next chapter.

basic.context: Elihu's final speech opens with one more claim to bring God's own perspective, then defends God's justice and disciplining purposes in suffering. But the speech's real power is in its second half, an extended, breathless description of a gathering thunderstorm, rain, snow, lightning, and wind, all obeying God without human understanding of how. By its end, the storm Elihu describes seems to have actually arrived, setting up God's appearance from the whirlwind in the very next chapter.
basic.hermeneutics: This speech functions as a hinge in the book: Elihu's arguments about justice largely repeat his earlier points, but his nature poetry does something new, it shifts the register from argument to awe, from explaining suffering to marveling at incomprehensible power. This shift prepares both Job and the reader for God's own speech, which will use exactly this kind of creation imagery rather than legal argument.
basic.originalLanguages: Elihu declares God gadol, great, beyond comprehension, and describes storms sent in chesed, His steadfast, loving purpose, whether for judgment or blessing. He calls Job to consider God's niphlaot, wonders, and concludes that all people rightly hold yirah, reverent fear, before such inaccessible majesty, the exact note God's own voice will strike beginning in chapter 38.
basic.typology: Elihu's storm, God's voice thundering, lightning obeying His command, anticipates the LORD's own appearance out of the whirlwind in the next chapter, and further anticipates the New Testament's pattern of divine appearances in storm and glory, culminating in Christ's authority over wind and waves, Mark 4:39-41.
basic.christ: Elihu marvels that no one can command the lightning or instruct the clouds, questions that find their startling answer in the Gospels, where the disciples ask in amazement, 'Who is this, that even the wind and the waves obey Him?' Mark 4:41, discovering in Jesus the very authority over creation that Elihu says belongs to God alone.
basic.god: God's power over weather is here presented not as arbitrary force but as chesed, loving devotion, purposeful whether in judgment or blessing; this same God is about to speak directly to Job, and Elihu's closing note of reverent fear rightly prepares the reader for that encounter.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 29 similarly pictures the LORD's voice in the thunder over the waters. Mark 4:39-41 has Jesus command the wind and sea, provoking the same awe Elihu describes. Psalm 147:8, 16-18 credits God with controlling rain, snow, and frost. Nahum 1:3 describes the LORD's way in the whirlwind and storm.
basic.oneStory: Elihu's closing speech moves from argument to awe, describing a God whose command over thunder, lightning, snow, and rain leaves humanity in reverent fear. This same authority over creation is claimed and displayed by Jesus Christ, who commands wind and waves with a word, revealing that the God Elihu describes from a distance has drawn near in a person.

context.literary: Elihu's longest and final speech, spanning two chapters, transitions from his familiar defense of divine justice into an extended nature hymn that grows in intensity until it functions almost as a literary bridge directly into God's own speech from the whirlwind in chapter 38, with no clear break in tone between Elihu's description of the storm and God's actual arrival in it.
context.historical: Ancient Near Eastern literature commonly associated thunderstorms with divine presence and voice, and Israel's own poetry, particularly the Psalms, frequently pictures the LORD's voice in thunder, Psalm 29:3-9; Elihu's hymn draws on this shared poetic tradition to build toward God's actual theophany.
context.canonical: This speech's storm imagery directly sets up Job 38:1, 'the LORD answered Job out of the whirlwind,' making Elihu's fourth speech function as an immediate literary preparation for, and near seamless transition into, God's own address to Job.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To conclude Elihu's contribution to the book by shifting from argument about divine justice toward wonder at divine power displayed in creation, preparing both Job and the reader for the different kind of answer God is about to give, one rooted in awe rather than explanation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Elihu's nature hymn is largely descriptive, marveling at real, observable phenomena, thunder, lightning, snow, as evidence of God's incomprehensible greatness; its implicit prescription is a call to humble, reverent fear rather than continued demand for a full explanation of suffering.

debates.question: Does Elihu's nature hymn in 36:26-37:24 actually anticipate God's own speech, or does it compete with it, making Elihu's role redundant once God speaks?

views.label: A genuine, Spirit guided preparation
views.case: The tonal and thematic overlap is so close, questions about who commands the lightning, the snow, the rain, that many read Elihu's final speech as a deliberate literary and theological bridge, softening Job for the greater impact of hearing these same questions from God's own mouth.

views.label: An overreach that God's speech then surpasses and corrects
views.case: Others note that Elihu claims to bring 'knowledge from afar' and to speak as 'one perfect in knowledge,' 36:3-4, a confidence God's own speech implicitly humbles by asking Job, not Elihu, whether he was present at creation, without ever validating Elihu's specific claims to insight.

debates.bearing: Both readings can be held together: Elihu's storm imagery genuinely prepares the reader for the tone of God's speech, even while Elihu's own claims to perfect knowledge are quietly set aside rather than confirmed once God speaks for Himself.

debates.question: Why does God not address Elihu directly in chapters 38-41 or rebuke him in 42:7-9 as He does the three friends?

views.label: Elihu's speech is essentially correct and needs no correction
views.case: God's silence toward Elihu, combined with his absence from the rebuke of the three friends, suggests his emphasis on divine transcendence and just discipline was fundamentally sound.

views.label: Elihu is simply eclipsed rather than affirmed
views.case: God's speech does not engage or confirm any of Elihu's specific arguments; it takes an entirely different approach, appealing to creation rather than to justice or discipline, suggesting Elihu is neither condemned nor vindicated, simply superseded by a greater voice.

debates.bearing: The text's silence about Elihu, both in God's speech and the epilogue, is genuinely ambiguous and best read as leaving Elihu's precise standing deliberately unresolved, a distinct voice neither fully rebuked nor fully endorsed.

study.originalLanguages: Elihu calls God gadol, great, beyond human knowledge, and describes the storm's power as an expression of chesed, His covenant faithfulness, whether the storm brings punishment or blessing. He commands Job to consider God's niphlaot, wonders, concluding that all people rightly hold yirah, reverent fear, before a majesty this far beyond human reach, the very posture God's own speech will now confirm as the appropriate response.

typology.type: God's voice thundering and lightning obeying His command, Job 37:2-5
typology.fulfillment: Christ commanding the wind and the sea, provoking the disciples' awestruck question, 'who is this,' Mark 4:39-41.

study.christ: Elihu asks who can command the lightning, instruct the clouds, or make the snow fall on command, questions meant to humble any claim to human mastery over creation. The Gospels answer these very questions directly: when Jesus rebukes the wind and says to the sea, 'Peace, be still,' and the wind and waves obey Him, His disciples are terrified and ask exactly Elihu's kind of question, 'who then is this, that even wind and sea obey Him?' Mark 4:39-41. The authority Elihu attributes to God alone is displayed in the person of Christ.
study.god: God's power over creation is not arbitrary display but chesed, loving devotion, purposeful whether in judgment or blessing, Elihu insists; this is the same God who is about to speak to Job directly, and this speech's rising awe rightly prepares the reader for an encounter that will move Job from argument to worship.

crossReferences.note: Pictures the voice of the LORD in the thunder over the waters, closely paralleling Elihu's storm imagery here.
Jesus commands the wind and waves, and His disciples respond with the same kind of awe Elihu calls for before God's power.
Credits God directly with sending rain, snow, and frost, echoing Elihu's description of these same phenomena.
The LORD answers Job 'out of the whirlwind,' the storm this speech has been building toward across chapters 36-37.

study.oneStory: Elihu's final speech leaves argument behind and turns to wonder, describing a God whose command over thunder, lightning, and snow leaves all people in reverent fear, setting the exact stage for God's own appearance in the whirlwind. That same divine authority over wind and wave is later displayed in the person of Jesus Christ, revealing that the transcendent God Elihu describes has drawn near enough to still a storm with a word.

sermon.title: Job: Rebuked in Suffering
sermon.why: Piper's sermon on Job 32:1-37:24 explicitly notes that 'toward the end of Elihu's speech a thunderstorm had gathered and filled him with awe,' observing that Elihu 'senses the approach of God in this storm' just before the LORD actually speaks out of the whirlwind, precisely the transition this chapter's nature hymn accomplishes.

quiz.q: How does Elihu's fourth speech differ in tone from his earlier speeches?

options.text: It shifts from confident argument about justice into an extended hymn of awe at God's power displayed in the weather.
options.note: Chapters 36:22-37:24 build an increasingly breathless description of a gathering storm rather than continuing direct argument.

options.text: It becomes far gentler and more comforting toward Job than before.
options.note: The tone shifts toward awe, though it remains challenging, questioning Job's ability to understand or command creation.

options.text: It abandons any discussion of God's justice entirely.
options.note: The speech opens by continuing to defend God's just, disciplining purposes, 36:5-15, before shifting to nature imagery.

options.text: It becomes shorter and less detailed than his previous speeches.
options.note: This is Elihu's longest single speech, spanning two full chapters.

quiz.q: What does Elihu's storm imagery in chapters 36-37 set up?

options.text: God's own appearance and speech to Job out of an actual whirlwind in chapter 38.
options.note: Job 38:1, 'the LORD answered Job out of the whirlwind,' follows almost seamlessly from Elihu's building storm description.

options.text: A literal historical flood that destroys the region.
options.note: The storm imagery functions poetically and theologically, preparing for God's speech, not narrating a literal flood event.

options.text: The final restoration of Job's wealth.
options.note: Restoration comes later, in chapter 42, after God's speeches and Job's response.

options.text: Zophar's return to give a final rebuttal.
options.note: Zophar never speaks again after chapter 20; this storm leads directly to God's own speech, not another human speaker.

quiz.q: What does Elihu mean by describing the storm as sent in God's 'loving devotion,' 37:13?

options.text: Even fearsome displays of power, whether for punishment or blessing, flow from God's steadfast, purposeful covenant character.
options.note: This ties even terrifying weather events to God's chesed, His faithful, loving purposes, not arbitrary cruelty.

options.text: Storms are always evidence of God's anger alone.
options.note: The verse explicitly says storms serve 'whether for punishment or for His land,' both purposes, not anger alone.

options.text: Weather events have no connection to God's character at all.
options.note: Elihu explicitly grounds the storm's purpose in God's own loving devotion.

options.text: God has no control over the weather.
options.note: The whole passage emphasizes God's direct command over every element of the storm.

quiz.q: How does this passage anticipate an episode in the Gospels?

options.text: It anticipates Jesus commanding the wind and sea, prompting His disciples to ask, 'who is this, that even wind and sea obey Him?'
options.note: Mark 4:39-41 records this scene, echoing exactly the kind of questions Elihu raises about who controls creation.

options.text: It anticipates the disciples calming a storm on their own.
options.note: In the Gospel account, Jesus, not the disciples, commands the storm to be still.

options.text: It anticipates John the Baptist's ministry in the wilderness.
options.note: The connection is to Christ's authority over creation, not to John the Baptist's ministry.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to any Gospel narrative.
options.note: The parallel to Jesus commanding the storm is a widely recognized typological connection.

quiz.q: What is the ambiguous status of Elihu at the end of his speeches, based on the book's later chapters?

options.text: He is neither rebuked alongside the three friends nor explicitly praised by God, leaving his exact standing deliberately unresolved.
options.note: God addresses Job directly in 38-41 and rebukes only Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar by name in 42:7-9, never mentioning Elihu either way.

options.text: God explicitly condemns Elihu as the worst speaker in the book.
options.note: Elihu is not named or condemned anywhere in God's speeches or the epilogue.

options.text: God explicitly praises Elihu above every other human speaker, including Job.
options.note: God's epilogue actually favors Job's speech over the friends'; Elihu receives no explicit commendation either.

options.text: Elihu is revealed to be a divine being rather than a human speaker.
options.note: The text presents Elihu as a young man, given a full human genealogy in 32:2, not a divine figure.

questions: Elihu moves from argument to wonder as the storm builds. Is there an area of your life where shifting from trying to explain your suffering to simply standing in awe of God might bring more peace?
Elihu says even fearsome storms flow from God's loving devotion, whether for judgment or blessing. How do you hold together God's power and His love when life feels stormy?
Christ commands wind and waves with a word, the very authority Elihu says belongs to God alone. How does knowing Jesus holds this authority change how you bring your fears to Him?
Elihu's closing words describe men rightly fearing God because of His inaccessible greatness. What would a deeper, more reverent fear of God look like in your daily life this week?

Elihu is neither fully rebuked nor fully praised in this book, a genuinely mixed and complicated voice. Where do you need to hold space for someone's counsel being partly helpful and partly overreaching, rather than sorting people into simply right or simply wrong?

translationNotes.note: 'Among the male shrine prostitutes' refers to a class of temple functionaries associated with pagan fertility cults, used here as an image of a shamefully premature death among the godless.
'Whether for punishment or for His land' captures the dual purpose Elihu assigns to storms, both corrective judgment and life giving blessing, both flowing from the same divine chesed.

'Out of the north He comes in golden splendor' may allude to ancient Near Eastern associations of the north with divine dwelling, building anticipation for the LORD's actual approach and speech in the following chapter.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 36:1-37:24. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
